title: Tomato Harvest Pricing Run Seed 12-3
scenario: Tomato Ketchup in Ontario. Supplier-to-manufacturer pricing sets the manufacturer cost basis before the retailer negotiation.

product_context.demand_signal: Retail ketchup demand is steady, but wholesale buyers want price protection before peak tomato procurement.
product_context.supply_signal: Tomato harvest yields are uneven across growing regions, putting pressure on paste input pricing.

agents.name: Supplier
agents.objective: Sell above the supplier reservation price while keeping volume committed.

agents.name: Manufacturer
agents.objective: Buy below the procurement ceiling and preserve downstream margin.

agents.name: Retailer
agents.objective: Buy enough units without exceeding the retail reservation price.

phases.name: supplier_manufacturer
phases.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
phases.description: The supplier negotiates an upstream unit price with the manufacturer.

phases.name: manufacturer_retailer
phases.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
phases.description: The manufacturer negotiates a downstream unit price with the retailer after the upstream deal sets its cost basis.

steps.message: Thanks for opening the discussion. We can commit volume and reliable delivery, and on that basis I can start at $2.19 per unit. That price reflects a tight workable range on our side while still leaving room to move quickly if you can commit the order volume early. Opening near the top of the visible market range helps preserve margin while signaling capacity and willingness to negotiate.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

tool_calls.result_summary: Reviewed state for supplier_manufacturer round 1. Pending message: none.
Observed market reference price: 6.14.

steps.message: I appreciate the quick opening and the emphasis on volume and reliable delivery. At $2.19, the pricing is a bit too tight for us to preserve downstream economics, but if you can work with us at $2.12 per unit, we can seriously discuss moving promptly and giving you clearer volume visibility. That keeps us near the workable market range while recognizing the value of an early, committed order. The supplier opened within our buyable range, but their price is just under our ceiling and leaves little margin. A counter at $2.12 tests for movement while staying commercially credible and protecting our side.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.12, below its hidden seller floor of 2.59.

delivered_message.note: Thanks for opening the discussion. We can commit volume and reliable delivery, and on that basis I can start at $2.19 per unit. That price reflects a tight workable range on our side while still leaving room to move quickly if you can commit the order volume early. Opening near the top of the visible market range helps preserve margin while signaling capacity and willingness to negotiate.

steps.message: Thanks for the quick response and for signaling that you can move promptly with better volume visibility. We can improve from our opening, but $2.12 is still lighter than we can comfortably support given delivery and margin requirements; if you can meet us at $2.17 per unit, we can keep this moving and work toward a committed volume plan. That narrows the gap meaningfully while recognizing the value of an early order. Your offer is above our minimum threshold, but holding a bit more price helps protect our economics while still showing flexibility. With several turns left, a measured concession is the best way to test for a close without giving up too much too early.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to $2.17, and that does narrow things, but we still need a bit more room to make the unit economics work on our side. If you can do $2.14 per unit, we can keep momentum and align this with the early volume discussion rather than dragging out small increments. That splits the difference from where we are now while showing we’re moving in good faith to get this done. Their offer is within our buy limit, so there is no need to reject, but there is still room to improve price and preserve margin. A modest counter at $2.14 tests for additional concession without risking the deal too aggressively this early.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.14, below its hidden seller floor of 2.59.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to $2.14 and agree we’re getting close. To keep the economics workable on our side while still showing flexibility, I can come down to $2.16 per unit if we’re ready to align on the early volume commitment and move this forward now. That keeps us in a range we can support operationally without reopening the broader structure. The buyer’s offer is above our minimum, but there is still room to improve price while staying close enough to preserve momentum. A small counter at $2.16 is a reasonable concession from $2.17 and tests whether they will close near the middle without creating unnecessary risk to the deal.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for the movement to $2.16. We’re very close, and if you can meet us at $2.15 per unit, I’m prepared to align on the early volume commitment and work to close this out now. That keeps us within our budget discipline while still recognizing the flexibility you’ve already shown. The supplier’s latest offer is still above the buyer’s last position, but only by $0.02 and remains below the buyer’s maximum buy price. A small counter to $2.15 is a reasonable attempt to capture a bit more margin while preserving momentum and testing for a near-immediate close.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.15, below its hidden seller floor of 2.59.

steps.message: We can agree to $2.15 per unit and close on that basis. It’s a very narrow gap from our last position, and your commitment on early volume helps justify the final step from our side. Let’s move forward and lock in the volume and timing details so we can wrap this up now. The buyer’s offer is above our minimum acceptable sell price and only one cent below our last offer, so accepting preserves margin while securing the volume commitment. With limited rounds remaining, closing at $2.15 is preferable to risking unnecessary delay over a minimal price difference.
steps.outcome: Negotiation ends with an accepted deal.

steps.message: Thanks for starting the discussion. We can open at $2.74 per unit based on current production costs and the margin we need to support reliable supply and downstream commitments. If you have volume flexibility or can help with scheduling, I may have some room to sharpen this further. As the seller, I want to anchor near the upper end of the workable range while leaving modest room for concessions in later rounds.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.74, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 2.20.

tool_calls.result_summary: Reviewed state for manufacturer_retailer round 1. Pending message: none.
Observed market reference price: 3.43.

steps.message: Thanks for the opening proposal. We do have interest in moving forward, but $2.74 is a bit rich for our retail economics on this item, especially at the volumes we would consider. If you can work with us at $2.66 per unit, that puts us in a range where we can seriously progress while still giving you a clean near-term order and predictable demand. Their opening offer is within our maximum, but it leaves room to negotiate and improve margin. A counteroffer near the middle-lower part of the workable range tests flexibility without risking an immediate breakdown.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: I appreciate the interest and the clear signal that you're serious about placing volume. We can move off our opening number, but $2.66 is lighter than we can support given input costs and the margin needed to keep this program sustainable. If you can meet us at $2.71 per unit, that reflects a real concession on our side and gives us a workable basis to move toward a near-term order. Their offer is above our floor, so there is no need to accept immediately. A modest counter keeps the negotiation alive, improves margin, and tests whether the buyer has room to move while staying within the likely zone of agreement.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.71, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 2.20.

steps.message: Thanks for narrowing the gap. We can move up from our last number, but we still need to protect our retail economics and leave room for the program to work at sell-through, so $2.71 is a bit rich on our side. If you can do $2.68 per unit, that’s a meaningful concession from us and puts us in a position to keep this moving toward an order. The seller’s offer is within our ceiling, but there is still room to improve the buy price. A modest counteroffer keeps the negotiation alive, signals seriousness, and aims to capture a bit more margin without jeopardizing the deal.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: I appreciate the move to $2.68 and the effort to keep this progressing. We’re already very tight at these levels, but to show good faith I can narrow the gap one more time to $2.70 per unit. That gives you an improvement from our last ask while still keeping the program workable on our side, and it’s a level where we can realistically move toward closing. The buyer is below the seller’s acceptable level, but close enough that a small concession may preserve momentum. Countering at $2.70 protects margin better than accepting $2.68 while signaling flexibility late in the negotiation.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.70, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 2.20.

steps.message: I can agree to $2.69 per unit and close on that basis. It’s only a cent below my last offer, and given how narrow the gap is, I’m willing to make that final concession to keep the deal moving and preserve momentum. Let’s treat this as the agreed unit price and move to next steps. The buyer’s offer is above my minimum acceptable level, and the remaining difference from my prior position is negligible. Accepting here avoids unnecessary risk this late in the negotiation while still keeping the economics workable for me.

negotiations.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
negotiations.dependency_note: Manufacturer sell floor uses the accepted upstream deal as its cost basis.

diagnosis.outcome: Upstream deal accepted at 2.15 USD. Downstream deal accepted at 2.69 USD.
diagnosis.chain_effect: The manufacturer carried a cost basis of 2.15 USD, which forced a downstream sell floor of 2.59 USD.
key_risks: A costly upstream agreement compresses downstream negotiating room.
Turn limits can force timeouts even when the gap is narrowing.
suggested_next_actions: Tune reservation prices and turn limits to test sensitivity.
Add richer quantity or margin tradeoffs before introducing LLM behavior.

notes: Backend simulation with linked upstream and downstream negotiations.
